blocks.text: Insider trading is buying or selling a security while in possession of MATERIAL, NONPUBLIC information (MNPI) — or passing it along. Material: a reasonable investor would want it before trading (earnings, mergers, FDA decisions, major contract wins or losses). Nonpublic: not yet broadly disseminated through a press release or filing. Both the tipper and the tippee who trades are liable, and so is anyone downstream who knew or should have known.

blocks.definition: Civil: disgorgement plus a penalty up to THREE TIMES the profit gained or loss avoided (treble damages), sued by the SEC under the Insider Trading and Securities Fraud Enforcement Act. Criminal: up to 20 years and multimillion-dollar fines per violation ($25 million for firms). Controlling firms face penalties for failing to supervise; bounties reward whistleblowers.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: A paralegal sees an unannounced merger at $68 against a $50 market price, tells her brother, and he buys calls, clearing $40,000. Both are liable — she never traded, but tipping MNPI is enough. His exposure: give back the $40,000, pay up to $120,000 more, and face criminal charges. Avoiding a LOSS counts identically: selling before bad news is the same violation.

An insider isn't just an officer: directors, employees, 10% owners, and anyone entrusted with MNPI (lawyers, printers, accountants, consultants — 'temporary insiders') count, along with family members they tell. Statutory insiders (officers, directors, 10% owners) also face Section 16 rules: they report their trades to the SEC, may never sell their company's stock short, and must return 'short-swing' profits made within six months. Firms must maintain information barriers ('Chinese walls') between investment banking and trading/research, plus watch and restricted lists to police them. Trading while in possession of MNPI is the violation — motive doesn't matter, and using MNPI to AVOID a loss counts.

blocks.definition: Creating a false appearance of activity or price: wash trades (buyer and seller are the same beneficial interest), matched orders (colluding accounts trade with each other), marking the close/open (painting the tape with late prints to set a price), spoofing/layering (fake orders entered to be canceled), and pump-and-dump rumor schemes (hype, then sell into the spike — a penny-stock classic).

blocks.text: Also prohibited: trading ahead of research reports, and spreading false rumors to move a price. Stabilization of a new issue by the managing underwriter is the ONE permitted form of price support — bids placed at or below the offering price, disclosed in the prospectus, to steady a new issue in its first days.

'Trades with no change in beneficial ownership' = wash trades. 'Buying at 3:59 to set the closing price' = marking the close. 'Orders entered with intent to cancel' = spoofing. Maximum civil insider-trading penalty: 3× the profit gained OR LOSS AVOIDED — the exam loves that phrase. Stabilization = the only legal manipulation.

blocks.definition: Entering a proprietary or personal order AHEAD of a customer's large order (or a pending research report) to profit from the price move it will cause. Prohibited whether the rep trades for himself, the firm, or a favored customer — the customer's order always goes first.

Excessive trading in a customer's account to generate commissions — measured against the customer's objectives and resources (quantitative suitability). Red flags: high turnover ratio, in-and-out trading, cost-to-equity ratios that make profit impossible. The trades can each be 'suitable' and the PATTERN still be churning. Reverse churning — parking a buy-and-hold client in a fee-based account — is the mirror-image abuse.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: A conservative retiree's $200,000 account turns over 12 times in a year, generating $38,000 in commissions. Every stock bought was blue-chip 'suitable' — but no strategy justifies the frequency. That's churning, and 'the customer never complained' is no defense. Discretionary accounts get extra supervisory scrutiny for exactly this reason.

blocks.caption: Selling away vs sharing vs borrowing
rows: Doing securities business OUTSIDE the firm
Written notice to firm; written approval required if compensated
Prior WRITTEN NOTICE to the firm
Only with firm + customer written approval, proportionate to contribution (proportionality waived for immediate family)
Only per firm procedures and narrow categories (immediate family, banks/lending institutions, registered colleagues)

blocks.title: Example — selling away
blocks.text: A rep's friend is raising money for a startup and offers him 5% of whatever he places with clients. Selling those unregistered shares to even one customer without the firm's written approval is selling away — a career-ending violation even if the startup succeeds. With no compensation, written notice would still be required and the firm could still say no.

More conduct violations to recognize on sight: guaranteeing a customer against loss (always prohibited — no one may promise performance or buy back a losing position); commingling customer assets with firm or personal assets; unauthorized trading (no discretion on file, no customer order); misusing customer funds; forging signatures or falsifying records; paying commissions to unregistered persons; and backing away from a firm quote. Gifts tied to business are capped at $100 per person per year under FINRA's gift rule; business entertainment where the rep attends is separate but must be reasonable and not conditioned on business.

blocks.definition: Buying securities in a cash account and paying for them with the proceeds of their own sale. Penalty: 90-day freeze — the account must trade on a cash-up-front basis. (Distinct from 'withholding/freeriding' in IPOs — Rule 5130's ban on industry insiders buying new-issue stock.)

blocks.text: Notice vs approval: OBA needs written NOTICE; selling away for compensation needs written APPROVAL. Sharing in a customer account is the rare thing that's ever allowed — approval plus proportionate share; guaranteeing against loss is NEVER allowed. Gift cap: $100 per person per year.

Anti-money-laundering (AML) rules chase dirty money through its three classic stages. PLACEMENT: illegal cash enters the financial system — the most detectable stage. LAYERING: rapid transfers, wires, and trades bury the trail. INTEGRATION: the funds re-emerge looking legitimate — invested in property, businesses, securities.

blocks.definition: The BSA is the foundational AML statute; FinCEN (Financial Crimes Enforcement Network, a Treasury bureau) administers it and receives the reports. The PATRIOT Act extended AML duties to broker-dealers: a written AML program approved by senior management, a designated AML compliance officer, ANNUAL independent testing, and ongoing employee training — the four pillars, plus customer due diligence (identifying beneficial owners of entity accounts).

blocks.caption: The two reports
rows: CASH transactions over $10,000 in one day (aggregated)

blocks.definition: Breaking cash deposits into sub-$10,000 pieces to dodge CTR filing — itself a federal crime even if the money is clean, and a classic SAR trigger. Three $4,000 cash deposits in a day aggregate to $12,000: CTR territory.

blocks.title: Example
blocks.text: A new customer wires in $95,000, buys and sells liquid stocks for a week with no apparent strategy, then wires the balance to a third-party account offshore. No single step is illegal — the PATTERN (rapid movement, no economic purpose, third-party disbursement) demands a SAR within 30 days, and no one may tell the customer it was filed.

Supporting rules: CIP identity verification at account opening (Chapter 12), OFAC list screening against sanctioned persons and countries (matches must be blocked and reported — OFAC is about sanctions, not suspicion), enhanced due diligence for foreign correspondent and private-banking accounts (with a hard ban on foreign shell banks), and FinCEN 314(a) information sharing between law enforcement and firms. Records of CTRs and SARs are kept FIVE years. Red flags worth naming: customers indifferent to cost or risk, accounts used as a pass-through, refusal to identify beneficial owners, and activity wildly inconsistent with the stated business.

Numbers to burn in: CTR = cash OVER $10,000, 15 days. SAR = $5,000+ and suspicious, 30 days, absolute secrecy. Placement → layering → integration. Structuring is a crime even if the money is clean. AML program pillars: written program, AML officer, annual independent audit, training.
